perturbation.original_text: Fox grants to Licensee a limited, non-exclusive right and license to distribute the following Wireless Products in connection with the Property "ICE AGE: THE MELTDOWN" in the United States: (A) 2 Scrat voicetones; (B) 2 John Leguizamo voicetones; and (C) 3 premium videos.
perturbation.changed_text: Fox grants to Licensee a limited, non-exclusive right and license to distribute the following Wireless Products in connection with the Property "ICE AGE: THE MELTDOWN" in the United States: (A) 2 Scrat voicetones; (B) 2 John Leguizamo voicetones; and (C) 3 premium videos, provided that the voicetones do not constitute "sound recordings" under copyright law.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the caveat that voicetones do not constitute 'sound recordings,' the contract introduces a contradiction with established copyright law, which generally considers voicetones as sound recordings.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification explicitly states that the voicetones may not constitute "sound recordings." However, copyright law (17 U.S.C. § 101) defines sound recordings broadly to include "works that result from the fixation of a series of musical, spoken, or other sounds." Voicetones clearly fall under this definition, creating a legal contradiction that could impact the enforceability of the licensing agreement.

Definitions Except as otherwise provided in this title, as used in this title, the following terms and their variant forms mean the following: An "anonymous work" is a work on the copies or phonorecords of which no natural person is identified as author. An "architectural work" is the design of a building as embodied in any tangible medium of expression, including a building, architectural plans, or drawings. The work inclu
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by stating that voicetones may not constitute 'sound recordings'. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both confirm that copyright law, specifically 17 U.S.C. § 101, defines sound recordings broadly, which would include voicetones. Scraped Law Snippet 2 directly references 17 USC 101: Definitions. This deviation from legally recognized definitions constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Royalty: In consideration of the rights granted to Licensee pursuant to this Amendment 2, Licensee shall pay to Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) From the first unit sold, Fox shall earn a royalty at the rate of ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Royalty: In consideration of the rights granted to Licensee pursuant to this Amendment 2, Licensee shall pay to Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) From the first unit sold, Fox shall earn a royalty at the rate of ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products, provided however, that Gross Receipts do not include payments from end users.
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that Gross Receipts do not include payments from end users, the contract attempts to redefine a common financial term in a way that contradicts typical accounting practices and may violate revenue recognition principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) - Revenue Recognition
perturbation.law_explanation: According to GAAP revenue recognition principles, specifically ASC 606, Gross Receipts would include payments from end users, because that is where the licensee generates revenue for providing the content. Explicitly excluding end-user payments from 'Gross Receipts' contradicts standard revenue recognition practices and could cause the contract to misrepresent income.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Gross Receipts' by excluding payments from end users. The Law Explanation states that this exclusion contradicts standard revenue recognition practices under GAAP, specifically ASC 606, as 'Gross Receipts' should include payments from end users. The absence of scraped law snippets does not diminish this contradiction, as the Law Explanation explicitly references established accounting principles. The modification in the changed text directly opposes the recognized definition of 'Gross Receipts' which forms the basis of revenue calculation.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall not be entitled to receive a share of any revenues and shall have no claim to any revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products which Fox distributes through its and its assigns' direct-to-consumer distribution channels.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall not be entitled to receive a share of any revenues and shall have no claim to any revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products which Fox distributes through its and its assigns' direct-to-consumer distribution channels, unless such distribution infringes on the Licensee’s pre-existing intellectual property rights.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the clause about pre-existing IP rights, the contract creates ambiguity regarding the scope of the revenue exclusion, which may conflict with established IP law concerning remedies for infringement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits
perturbation.law_explanation: While the original text clearly states that the licensee has no right to any revenues from direct-to-consumer distribution, the added clause creates an exception if that distribution infringes on the licensee’s intellectual property rights. According to 17 U.S.C. § 504, a copyright owner is entitled to recover the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the infringement, and any profits of the infringer that are attributable to the infringement. So, although the licensee may not have a contractual right, they would have a legal right to recover those profits if the licensee's IP rights were infringed upon.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 504 - Remedies for infringement: Damages and profits U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Except as otherwise provided by this title, an infringer of copyright is liable for either— (1) the copyright owner’s actual damages and any additional profits of the infringer, as provided by subsection (b); or (2) statutory damages, as provided by subsection (c). (b) Actual Damages and Profits.— The copyright owner is entitled to recover the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the infringement, and any profits of the infringer that are attributable to the infringement and are not taken into account in computing the actual damages. In establishing the infringer’s profits, the copyright owner is required to present proof only of the infringer’s gross revenue, and the infringer is required to prove his or her deductible expenses and the elements of profit attributable to factors other than the copyrighted work. (c) Statutory Damages.— (1) Except as provided by clause (2) of this subsection, the copyright owner may elect, at any time before final judgment is rendered, to recover, instead of actual damages and profits, an award of statutory damages for all infringements involved in the action, with respect to any one work, for which any one infringer is liable individually, or for which any two or more infringers are liable jointly and severally, in a sum of not less than $750 or more than $30,000 as the court considers just. For the purposes of this subsection, all the parts of a compilation or derivative work constitute one work. perturbation.justification: The modified contract language introduces an exception to the revenue exclusion if the distribution infringes on the licensee's IP rights. This contradicts the original agreement, which stated the licensee had no claim to any revenues. The Law Explanation states that according to 17 U.S.C. § 504, a copyright owner is entitled to recover damages and profits from infringement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that "The copyright owner is entitled to recover the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the infringement, and any profits of the infringer that are attributable to the infringement." Therefore, the addition of the clause creates a legal contradiction, because despite the original text the licensee would have legal rights to the profits if their IP rights were infringed upon.
